subtitle_main: For ethical and declarative transparency of AI use in content creation
section_initiative_heading: An initiative born from the field
section_initiative_text: This project didn't come from a regulatory committee, but from a dual observation, both professional and personal. On the professional side, I saw confusion take hold, how to value substantive work when the shadow of automation looms over every line? On the personal side, as a user, I felt this need to know who is really speaking to me.
section_constat_intro: Before launching this project, I searched to see if such an approach already existed. There are major initiatives, but none answered my specific idea precisely: to qualify the human relationship with the tool.
constat_item1: The EU AI Act: a global legal framework that imposes transparency obligations (notably Article 50), but without providing an accessible roadmap for creators' daily lives
constat_item2: AI Transparency Protocol: a technical compliance solution via a JSON file (/.well-known/), perfect for website audits but centered on regulatory response rather than the creative process
constat_item3: Content Authenticity Initiative (CAI): a robust standard to prove technical file origin via IPTC and C2PA standards
constat_item4: Trust Identity Protocol (TIP): focused on identity certification to distinguish human from bot, essential for combating impersonation but silent on content production method
constat_item5: OETP (Open Ethics): a comprehensive disclosure protocol for AI systems ethical audit, designed for corporate governance more than editorial readability
constat_item6: Declare AI: a social tagging initiative, very much in the spirit of citizen action but without the crucial distinction between substance and form
constat_item7: TRP (Trustable Reporting Protocol): an IETF standards project for technical machine-to-machine reporting, situated at the network infrastructure level
constat_conclusion1: This list is not exhaustive, so fast-moving is the topic right now. These tools provide technical or regulatory infrastructure, they allow anchoring proofs or validating identities. However, they often remain complex and don't answer a simple question: "To what extent was the human involved in the design of this content?"
constat_conclusion2: TCP/UP fills a gap here. It doesn't seek to prove technically, but to declare sincerely. Where other protocols stop at AI detection, TCP/UP values the "user's share" by distinguishing intellectual intention from technical execution.
hw_definition: Definition: Human-Washing consists of presenting content as the fruit of human thought, when it was largely generated or guided by AI, without the reader being clearly informed. This practice, whether deliberate or by omission, blurs the line between authentic creation and automated production.
hw_symptoms_intro: This phenomenon creates three major symptoms:
hw_symptom1: Widespread suspicion: The public doubts the origin of every text, image or video, eroding trust in all digital content
hw_symptom2: Devaluation of expertise: Substantive work, research and human reflection are drowned in synthetic mass, with no way to distinguish them.
hw_symptom3: Method opacity: Without clear indication, the reader can no longer distinguish the authentic author from automated content, making informed evaluation of information impossible
hw_solution: TCP/UP proposes a simple solution: voluntary transparency. By openly declaring the respective roles of humans and AI, each creator restores trust and values their true contribution.
section_impasse_heading: Why binary 'AI or not AI' labeling is a dead end
impasse_intro: The current debate on digital creation suffers from a binary vision that doesn't reflect field reality. Imposed labeling systems (by platforms or detection algorithms) fail for three fundamental reasons:
impasse_reason1: Unfair stigmatization: A single 'AI Content' label makes no distinction between an author using AI to correct grammar (HCA) and one who generates an entire article with a click (AIC). This opacity creates widespread suspicion that pushes creators to hide their tools for fear of discredit, paradoxically reinforcing lack of transparency
impasse_reason2: The gray zone of hybridization: Modern creation is a spectrum. By forcing a choice between '100% Human' or '100% AI', we deny the existence of intellectual co-construction (ACE). This binary is incapable of valuing human intention when it pilots machine execution
impasse_reason3: Technical impotence: No cryptographic proof or watermark will ever guarantee total absence of AI infallibly. The game of 'cat and mouse' between detectors and generators is a race lost from the start
section_issue_heading: The only way out: good faith declaration
issue_paragraph1: Since no technical lock will ever prevent an ill-intentioned person from lying about their process, the solution doesn't lie in proof, but in commitment.
issue_paragraph2: TCP/UP bets on responsibility. By proposing a scale of 5 nuanced labels, we move away from distrust to enter the age of voluntary transparency. Anyone adopting TCP/UP isn't trying to prove they're not lying, they choose to expose their method out of respect for their audience.
issue_paragraph3: Ultimately, faced with the unverifiable, only the author's declared sincerity can restore trust. If ill faith is always possible, good faith must finally have a language to express itself.
section_boussole_heading: TCP/UP: the ethical compass and reading grid
boussole_intro:
TCP/UP's approach is declarative, readable and based on nuance. Where other devices seek to detect, trace or authenticate, TCP/UP primarily seeks to make understandable the production method of content.
TCP/UP doesn't replace technical standards like CAI, IPTC or C2PA. It positions itself as a complementary editorial and semantic layer, centered on a simple question: what was the human's true role in this content?
boussole_item1: An editorial compass: where technology can signal that content involved an AI tool, TCP/UP allows the author to declare the nature of this intervention. For example: human substance, assisted wording with the HCE label
boussole_item2: A universal reading grid: with just 5 labels (HUC, HCA, HCE, ACE, AIC), any reader immediately understands the nature of human intervention
boussole_usage: It can be used for any textual content: from simple blog posts to complex technical reports.
boussole_quote: "Faced with this fog and the lack of a tool suited to this scope, I chose to launch this independent citizen initiative. My goal: create a simple and honest tool to empower creators and bring clarity to readers."
